title: Movie Theaters Are Reopening. Is America Ready? Plus: RZA!
format: Sean and Amanda talk through whether it's safe or even worth it to go back to theaters as Unhinged and the New Mutants reopen 43 states and Tenet looms, then look ahead to a fall slate mostly headed for streaming. Plus: RZA on directing Cutthroat City.

segments.heading: Movie theaters are reopening. Is America ready?
segments.note: the weekend of August 21st, and the fall slate behind it

films.title: Unhinged
films.note: The Russell Crowe road-rage thriller Solstice picked to reopen theaters. Sean saw it at home and found it a perfectly adequate exploitation picture that already feels like VOD; the choice is a savvy attention-grab if not a public-health one.

films.title: Tenet
films.note: Nolan's, dated September 3rd with no major releases behind it for a month, a deliberate buffer to see how reopening goes. Sean and Amanda, both in California, can't see it and dread being left out of the conversation.

films.title: The New Mutants
films.note: Josh Boone's long-delayed haunted-institution X-Men spinoff, opening August 28th. Orphaned by the Fox-Disney merger and contractually owed a theatrical release; Sean is morbidly fascinated by a franchise relaunch that can never go anywhere.

films.title: Bill & Ted Face the Music
films.note: The third Bill & Ted, also August 28th, in theaters and on PVOD. Sean already spoke to Keanu Reeves and Alex Winter and calls it a fun one for fans.

films.title: Mulan
films.note: Going straight to Disney+ for a $29.99 premium on September 4th, the cheaper-and-safer home alternative in the streaming-vs-theater math.

films.title: I'm Thinking of Ending Things
films.note: One of the three big Labor Day releases, headed to Netflix rather than theaters.

films.title: Palm Springs
films.note: The recent example of a movie that built a community without theaters: everyone Sean knew liked it and it worked as a balm, doing the talk-theory and the calm-down thing at once.

films.title: Joker
films.note: Amanda's stand-in for the have-to-see event she'd happily skip if the monoculture shrinks.

films.title: Antebellum
films.note: Pulled out of theaters entirely by Lionsgate and sent to premium VOD.

films.title: The King's Man
films.note: The Kingsman prequel then holding a September 18th theatrical date Sean doubts will stick.

films.title: Wonder Woman 1984
films.note: Then dated October 2nd; Amanda's favorite of the superhero blockbusters and the start of her stacked October.

films.title: On the Rocks
films.note: Sofia Coppola's Bill Murray / Rashida Jones father-daughter movie for Apple TV+; Amanda's most anticipated, and she's been sitting on a bottle of Sofia rose to prove it.

films.title: Death on the Nile
films.note: Kenneth Branagh's Poirot follow-up, shot on a soundstage rather than on location. Amanda has continuity concerns; Sean, having liked the original, isn't sure he needs it.

films.title: Death on the Nile (1978)
films.note: The Ustinov version Sean started during quarantine and fell asleep in, despite a cast that includes Angela Lansbury, David Niven, Mia Farrow, Bette Davis. He means to finish it before Branagh's.

films.title: Murder on the Orient Express
films.note: Branagh's first Poirot; Sean and Amanda saw it in Pasadena with the over-80 crowd. Praised for the Irwin Allen approach of stacking the cast with stars.

films.title: The Trial of the Chicago 7
films.note: Sorkin, acquired by Netflix off Paramount; a cornerstone of Amanda's October.

films.title: Mank
films.note: Fincher's, coming to Netflix by year's end, undated but promised for October. Sean's most anticipated: he figures it's for one million very excited people rather than the usual audience of 100 million.

films.title: Rebecca
films.note: Ben Wheatley's remake of the Hitchcock, also headed to Netflix.

films.title: The Devil All the Time
films.note: Antonio Campos's, coming straight to Netflix, part of the stack that might carry the mass-cultural moment theaters can't.

films.title: Cutthroat City
films.note: RZA's New Orleans heist thriller, set in the Ninth Ward after Katrina, one-third shot on location among houses that still bore FEMA X's. He reframed the core as aspiration turning to desperation, rewrote a lead cop as a woman for Eiza Gonzalez, and got heat-check turns from Ethan Hawke and Rob Morgan alongside Shameik Moore, Demetrius Shipp Jr., Wesley Snipes, T.I. and Terrence Howard.

films.title: The Man with the Iron Fists
films.note: His directorial debut, where he learned to hire the production designer and scouting team first to build the world.

films.title: Love Beats Rhymes
films.note: The movie he took to prove his storytelling range beyond kung fu, after Taraji P. Henson assumed he only did martial-arts pictures.

films.title: Set It Off
films.note: Named alongside the classic heist films he studied for Cutthroat City.

films.title: Dog Day Afternoon
films.note: A heist he singled out for its vibe.

films.title: Inside Man
films.note: Another heist he rated a good watch in prep.

films.title: 12 Angry Men
films.note: How far back he went while soaking up single-location tension.

films.title: Die Hard
films.note: The hundred-million-dollar tier he knew he wasn't competing with, which pushed him to keep the stakes at a $100K score.

films.title: Scarface
films.note: His example of a criminal you love anyway, the trick being to make the audience care about men with no other options.

films.title: Baby Driver
films.note: Where he first saw Eiza Gonzalez as a stunner and then pitched her the un-glamorous cop role.

films.title: Flag of Iron
films.note: The Shaw Brothers film he'd just watched with his 14-year-old son.

films.title: Five Deadly Venoms
films.note: A martial-arts staple his son grew up seeing in the background as RZA rewatched it.

films.title: How the West Was Won
films.note: An assignment from his son's film class.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in the West
films.note: Sergio Leone, another of the son's class assignments.

films.title: Minority Report
films.note: Also on the film-class syllabus they worked through together.

films.title: Fist of the White Lotus
films.note: The kung-fu title he introduced his son to next.

films.title: Torn Curtain
films.note: The Hitchcock with Paul Newman and Julie Andrews that blew his mind during quarantine after he'd kept setting it aside.

films.title: Marnie
films.note: The other Hitchcock he'd been putting off and finally watched, to the same effect.

films.title: The Aeronauts
films.note: What he watched the night before the interview.

films.title: At Eternity's Gate
films.note: The Willem Dafoe Van Gogh film he'd seen two nights prior, from Dafoe's Oscar run.

Ads: Drive Sober or Get Pulled Over (NHTSA)
